nuggets.summary: To validate the trucking idea before raising money, Dan Lewis skipped desk research entirely and did guerrilla fieldwork: he searched LinkedIn for every person he could find in the trucking industry and cold-emailed them, which led to chain introductions ("they'd introduce me to three other people"). He physically visited operations — he got kicked out of a Whole Foods warehouse in the Sodo neighborhood of Seattle for walking in unannounced — and staked out truck stops in Marysville and other locations south of Seattle, approaching truckers walking between their rigs and the diner to ask what they liked and disliked about their work. This six-week immersion, done before he had any data, was specifically designed to compress the time between quitting his job and having a credible enough story to raise money and recruit engineers.
nuggets.quote: So, that was just honestly walking into warehouses. I went on LinkedIn and looked up every single person in the trucking industry and cold in-mailed them. And then they would talk to me and they'd introduce me to three other people. Kept doing that. Got kicked out of the Whole Foods warehouse down in Soto.

nuggets.summary: While simultaneously researching a second idea (mobile retail for college students), Lewis bought a stack of $5 Starbucks gift cards, printed a paper questionnaire on a clipboard, and deliberately picked out a shirt that "didn't make me look sketchy" before showing up at community colleges to interview students in person. His interview technique was to have students hand over their phones and walk him through exactly which shopping apps they used and how — a hands-on, physical-artifact research method rather than a survey tool or focus group.
nuggets.quote: So, I went and bought a bunch of Starbucks $5 gift cards, got a clipboard, printed, like, a questionnaire, put on a shirt that didn't make me look sketchy. And then went to a bunch of community colleges and just interviewed tons of students, you know, and just did the same thing. About how do you shop? Show me your phone. How do you shop on your phone?

nuggets.summary: Lewis found that the specific value of his six weeks of guerrilla customer research wasn't the data itself — he explicitly says he "didn't have data yet" — but the anecdotes it gave him. Having a first-hand story ready for every question an investor could ask shifted his pitch from "I have an idea" to a narrative backed by real conversations, and he credits this shift with changing the substance of his fundraising meetings, not just their polish.
nuggets.quote: It changed the entire conversation from I have an idea to every time there was a question, I could pull an anecdote. I didn't have data yet, but I had anecdotes. And that changed all of the fundraising conversations.

nuggets.summary: When advising younger entrepreneurs, Lewis pushes back on the instinct to validate an idea with friends and treat their encouragement as a green light. His view, sharpened by his own experience, is that friends are actually poor validation sources because they'll be nice rather than honest, so the real test is deliberately seeking out strangers instead. He also notes an unexpected payoff from doing this: many of the strangers he cold-approached during his six weeks of research (truckers, warehouse workers) later became paying customers, and having been the "crazy guy" who showed up cold made it an easier sell once he had a working product to show them, rather than a cold pitch.
nuggets.quote: So friends are bad, bad customer validation. Don't talk to your friends. Talk to your non-friends. Those people I talked to early on became customers later, which is great. Because then you can go back to them and say, hey, man, you're so hopeful to me. You're this crazy guy, exactly. Thank you so much for your help. Why don't I show you what we actually built?

nuggets.summary: As a concrete marker of Convoy's early trajectory, Lewis states that in roughly its first year as a real company the business grew to about 45 employees and raised approximately $19 million in funding — figures he offers as evidence of how compressed and fast his transition into the CEO role actually was, tying the pace of headcount and capital growth directly to how disorienting the experience felt.
nuggets.quote: I've been a CEO of a company that has gone from zero to, you know, 45 people or so, and we've raised about $19 million in just about a year.

nuggets.summary: Reflecting on becoming a first-time CEO, Lewis says he could not have predicted what the role would actually feel like, no matter how much he prepared mentally beforehand — a realization that led him to a broader conclusion about empathy itself: that people generally cannot truly understand an experience until they have lived it themselves, in any category. He draws a direct parallel to becoming a parent, where he had the same experience of prior expectation failing to match reality, and uses that comparison to argue there's no useful analogy for what being a CEO is actually like.
nuggets.quote: It actually made me think at one point, I was like, I don't think humans are very good at empathy. I don't think you can actually understand what someone's really going through until you do it yourself in any category. And when I had kids, the first time I was like, okay, I never could have expected what that was like. And then doing this, like, couldn't have predicted that.

nuggets.summary: Facing an actual, undecided choice at age 22 between going into teaching (Spanish) or management consulting after returning from a year in Chile, in a weak 2003 economy in the aftermath of the tech bubble, Lewis says a Yale dean gave him a specific decision rule rather than generic career advice: since starting in consulting keeps the door open to teaching later, but starting in teaching makes a later pivot into consulting much less likely, the higher-"option value" path was to consult first. Unsure which path he actually wanted, Lewis used this asymmetry — not passion or interest — as the tiebreaker, and says this framework was the actual deciding factor in a real choice he was otherwise ambivalent about.
nuggets.quote: he basically said, look, let me teach you this thing called option value... if you go do management consulting right now, you can have, the odds of you being able to go be a teacher later are really high... If you go do a teaching right now, the odds of being able to go into management consulting later are really low. So, if you're not sure which one you want to do, pick the one that has more option value.

nuggets.summary: Convoy's early speed wasn't a generic "move fast" startup platitude - it was a deliberate response to a specific, dated risk. In 2015 there was real market speculation that the truck/freight funding environment would dry up or change dramatically in 2016, so Lewis and his co-founders built an explicit work-back plan from company founding in April to closing a credible Series A within the same year - about eight months from starting the company. The urgency came from treating the funding window as closing, not from an assumption that speed itself is always the smart path.
nuggets.quote: the market, back in 2015, there was a lot of speculation that the funding market would dry up or change dramatically in 2016... we created a work back plan that involved us getting to the point we could go credibly get a series A done in 2015 with basically eight months from finding the company.

nuggets.summary: To make the Series A raise credible, Convoy set an explicit, minimal operating milestone rather than a vague growth goal: reach 100 shipments by a set date, even before they had a real product. The number wasn't chosen for its own sake - it was calibrated as the smallest volume that would let the team understand its own unit economics and tell a credible story to investors about scaling the business further.
nuggets.quote: we're in trucking. We were new. Like, we need a hundred shipments, um, by this date. We didn't have a product. Like, we need to get a hundred shipments at least so we can understand our business enough to go have a really credible story about how to take it to the next level.

nuggets.summary: Lewis asked Jeff Bezos (an Amazon investor of Convoy, and Lewis's former employer) how Amazon had arrived at its company values, expecting a top-down founding decision. Instead Bezos said Amazon deliberately did not set values at founding - they let the company operate and observe what behaviors were actually driving success, then formalized those into stated values only once the company reached roughly 90-100 employees. Convoy consciously copied this sequencing: rather than write values on day one, they waited until close to their one-year mark, looked back at what had actually made them successful, and wrote values from that evidence.
nuggets.quote: we didn't just go set the values. We, the company developed and then we saw what was working, right? And then we thought about, okay, what's working, now let's capture those and encapsulate those values into a set of things that we actually live by... I think he said it was around like 90 or 100 people or something like that... we waited a little while, kind of towards about the year mark, we started working on our values.

nuggets.summary: Lewis contrasts how goal-setting actually worked day-to-day at Amazon versus Microsoft. At Amazon, individual team goals (a 10- or 20-person team's goals) rolled all the way to the top without being aggregated into broader summary goals - meaning senior leadership, including the CEO, was directly tracking on the order of hundreds of individual team goals rather than a handful of high-level ones. At Microsoft, by contrast, goals were aggregated layer by layer until the CEO tracked only 15-20 rolled-up goals, which created a "watered-down" effect where an individual team member's own specific goal became invisible to leadership, reducing the felt accountability of hitting it.
nuggets.quote: the goals that a lot of individual teams have, maybe a 10-person team or a 20-person team has, roll all the way up to the top. They don't get aggregated. So it's not like the CEO has 20 goals that he cares about. He's reading like 500 goals... And at Microsoft, for example... these seven goals were rolling to this goal. And then at that level, the other four goals were rolling to this goal. And then the CEO would have like 15 or 20 goals.

nuggets.summary: Lewis uses Amazon's product-reviews team as a cautionary example of what happens when an organization conflates the problem it's solving with the solution it built. The original problem was customer confidence — how to give online shoppers the confidence to buy without a salesperson or the ability to touch the product — and the solution was reviews. But because the team was literally named "the reviews team," every subsequent hire and goal became about making the review metric itself (more reviews, more countries, more platforms, higher quality, less fraud) go up and to the right, rather than revisiting the underlying confidence problem. It took a deliberate return to the original problem, years later, to unlock features like Q&A ("ask a question") and fit guidance ("does this run big or small") that had been sitting unbuilt because the team had structurally forgotten the problem in favor of optimizing its existing solution. Lewis explicitly imports this as an operating discipline at Convoy: when a team is fixated on improving a solution, someone should be able to ask "what was the problem?" and challenge whether the current solution still serves it.
nuggets.quote: But then they called the team reviews. And then everyone they ever hired from there on out was hired to the reviews team. And so every goal they set was how do we make reviews go up and to the right? And more reviews in more countries and more platforms with higher quality and less fraud and everything like that... if you forget about the problem you're originally solving and you focus on improving the solution, you may actually miss like five other amazing opportunities... now you have things like ask a question on a product for Amazon to get an answer from another customer. That came out of this discussion... Those all happened in the last... few years. And that is something I'm taking to heart at Convoy.

nuggets.summary: Building Convoy's MVP took about four months end-to-end — the engineering team was assembled in April/May and the product launched with real customers between the end of August and beginning of September of the same year. Lewis's dominant fear throughout that build wasn't generic startup anxiety but a specific one: that after months of engineering work nobody would actually use the product, and that everyone who told him during development that they needed it had just been being polite. That fear only resolved the moment their actual first customer — Guided Products, a Kent, WA-based printed-materials company — completed its first real shipment on the platform (a couple of pallets of paper moved between two Puget Sound facilities).
nuggets.quote: we spent four months building the product. We got the engineering team together April, May, and then built the MVP to launch with customers in the end of August and beginning of September. And until we actually had that first shipment, I just was like, man, what if nobody ever uses this?... My biggest fear is that nobody would ever use it to ship a product... everybody else lied to me along the way that they needed it.

nuggets.summary: Convoy assumed, by analogy to Uber, that they could just give truck drivers phones to solve the technology-adoption problem, and initially built only an Android app (roughly 85% of truckers had smartphones at the time, so anyone with an iPhone was simply issued a physical Android device until an iOS app existed). The Uber analogy broke down because an Uber driver's phone usage is fully real-time - they're either on a trip or idle waiting for the next one - whereas a trucker is usually mid-job when a dispatch notification for tomorrow's job comes in, so they default to their own phone for calls with their dispatcher/manager and won't glance at the Convoy-issued device until the end of the day. Because the work wasn't real-time, the assumption that "give them a device" would drive engagement the way it did for Uber turned out to be wrong, and Convoy had to scramble to build an iOS app after the fact to recover. Dan frames this as the clean example of a plausible cross-industry analogy (Uber's model) actively misleading early product strategy.
nuggets.quote: Turns out that you can't just give drivers phones because they don't, unlike, we thought Uber gave drivers phones, so why don't we give drivers phones?... Whereas a trucker is doing a job right now, but getting a notification for a job that starts tomorrow from convoy, right? So they're going to use their whatever phone they already have to have phone calls with their manager and their dispatcher as the default, and they're not going to look at the other phone until the end of the day.

nuggets.summary: Convoy's MVP shipped with both a driver/carrier app and a shipper-facing web experience - i.e., they built the primary interface for both sides of their marketplace at once, when in hindsight one side was clearly more critical and the other could have been handled manually behind the scenes while the harder problem was solved with automation first. Dan's retrospective advice, drawn directly from this experience, is to identify the two most important of, say, five things that need to happen for a transaction to occur, build only those really well with technology, and do the rest manually so engineering and management bandwidth isn't fragmented across too many unproven scenarios at once. He ties this directly to a lesson from his machine-learning background: solve the use case manually with humans first to nail the scenario, then automate it, rather than automating before you actually know what "right" looks like.
nuggets.quote: I may have even, for the MVP, done less. Like, really, like, just picked one thing to automate and to build with technology, and then done that one, maybe just the carrier experience, and then everything else manually.

nuggets.summary: While racing to ship Convoy's MVP in just four months, one of the engineers, Alex, pushed the team with a line Dan still quotes: get something out the door so they could actually learn what they should be building, rather than trying to design it perfectly upfront. That instinct paid off unusually well - the end-to-end, self-service, fully automated version they shipped turned out to be close to the right product, and roughly two and a half months after launch they were able to close their seed fundraise on the strength of the traction it generated. Dan attributes the low-risk outcome not to luck but to the amount of field research (data, numbers, direct customer conversations) that had already been done before the MVP was built, even while acknowledging real risk still remained.
nuggets.quote: Let's just get this out the door so we can figure out what the hell, you know, we should actually be building... Turned out we actually, I think we're really fortunate, and the thing we built was end-to-end... two and a half months later, we were able to go do the fundraise.

nuggets.summary: Lewis believes investors evaluating an early-stage founder are silently checking three things: whether the founder is fully committed (not moonlighting), whether the opportunity is credible, and — the hardest to prove — whether the founder can actually assemble a team to execute, since most early conversations are just an idea plus a promise to hire later. Convoy's specific tactic for de-risking that third question was recruiting five or six engineers to work on the product part-time, for free, before the raise even started, then putting their names, faces, and willingness to serve as references directly into the pitch deck as proof the team already existed and was already building, not hypothetical. Dan says this, more than the story or the deck craft itself, was what actually differentiated their fundraising from other pitches investors were considering — visible, named engineering commitment substituted for generalist investors' inability to independently judge technical feasibility.
nuggets.quote: we had five or six people working on this who were engineers and helping us when we wanted to go raise money that was the thing that made all the difference the other stuff I mentioned is super important having the engineering team doing it and committed and like their names on a slide and their pictures and like them willing to be references changed it

nuggets.summary: Lewis used an explicit investor-pitch framework distinguishing "linear" businesses, where consistent effort and thoughtfulness predictably produce results (e.g. trucking/logistics), from "binary" or nonlinear/scale-dependent businesses that fail entirely unless they hit massive scale (e.g. gaming, social/messaging networks, most two-sided consumer marketplaces). He deployed this framing deliberately to argue Convoy was a safer, more derisked bet than other opportunities investors were weighing, since most generalist investors couldn't independently judge trucking versus gaming versus social networking. As evidence, he pointed to Path, a social network that reached medium scale but never broke out, arguing binary categories don't really support mid-sized outcomes — you scale or you die, whereas a linear-effort business rewards the work and research put in.
nuggets.quote: is this a linear business or a nonlinear business is this a binary business or is this a business that has sort of a gradient ... there are a lot of businesses that really aren't going to be successful unless they're massive unless they really nail it like gaming is one of the ones that kind of falls into that department ... whereas there are other businesses that are much more linear and effort and I think they're less risky ... trucking for example and logistics I felt like it was less risky because I felt like we could put the energy in and do the homework ... you don't see a lot of like mid-sized social networks like path right kind of was medium sized and it's done it's done it has to be massive or it doesn't really go

nuggets.summary: Convoy never considered bootstrapping, and Lewis's reasoning was tied to a precise, narrow window of technological readiness: truck driver smartphone penetration, data plan availability, and GPS accuracy had only recently crossed a threshold that made a trucking marketplace app viable, whereas just a few years earlier — especially in rural areas — that infrastructure simply didn't exist. Believing this created a limited first-mover window against incumbents, he treated speed as directly convertible into money, so raising outside capital was chosen specifically to buy pace rather than for capital's own sake. This is a specific, dated causal chain (device penetration plus data plans plus GPS accuracy hitting a tipping point) rather than a generic "raise money to move fast" claim.
nuggets.quote: we didn't consider bootstrapping ... couldn't do this the reason is that so we looked at this business as hey technology just shifted truck drivers didn't have smartphones two years ago ... and gps wasn't accurate enough everywhere just even a few years ago right especially in rural areas ... we believe that there was a window of opportunity to go after this industry and we timed it really well timing is a huge part of any startup ... time equals money to accelerate us getting to market

nuggets.summary: Right after Convoy closed its seed round, investors gave Lewis a specific, non-obvious piece of advice: write the Series B pitch deck immediately, before even fundraising for Series A, on the logic that founders who don't work backward from that future story now "may never" get there. He acted on it literally — after the seed round, the team wrote out what the Series A story needed to say and then reverse-engineered which customer diversity, shipment volume, and territory/team milestones they needed to produce that story, rather than letting traction passively dictate the pitch later. This is a concrete process detail — writing a future round's deck as a present-day planning tool — not generic "have a fundraising plan" advice.
nuggets.quote: some of the best advice I got even when we were raising our series A as soon as we were done some of our investors when I was talking about it they said write your series B deck right now like if you believe you're going to do a series B you may never do it I mean we probably will but go write your series B deck

nuggets.summary: To build Convoy's Series A hiring plan, Lewis drew a literal two-by-two grid mapping the credibility gaps he needed to close — demand-side marketplace expertise, supply-side marketplace expertise, and deep trucking-industry expertise — and wrote the actual name of every candidate he was recruiting that summer into the corresponding cell, while building the operations team in parallel so it would be in place for the September launch. The point wasn't just filling roles: he needed those specific hires visibly in progress so he could credibly tell Series A investors a story about de-risked execution capability. This is a reproducible artifact-level tactic (a literal grid with real candidate names on it), not generic "build a strong team" advice.
nuggets.quote: I actually drew a grid it was like a two by two I was like I need to have people who understand you know demand side of the business in a marketplace the supply part of business in the marketplace really understand marketplace dynamics and really understand the trucking industry ... I took every person I was speaking with during my recruiting during the summer when we were building the product I was trying to get the operations team built in parallel so when we went to launch in September we'd have that already and we wrote everyone's name that we were recruiting on that sheet

nuggets.summary: Convoy deliberately did not define company values at founding. In week one, Lewis and Grant took the first five engineers on a retreat to discuss who they wanted to be as a company, concluded "we don't know who we are yet," and consciously tabled the exercise. They returned to it roughly a year later, once the company had grown to about 30 people and Lewis could still talk to everyone end-to-end, extracting values (like "fall in love with problems not solutions" and "know why") from behaviors people already exhibited and liked, rather than inventing aspirational ones upfront. This runs against the common startup-culture advice to set values on day one — Lewis's position was that values can't be credibly articulated until there's enough lived organizational behavior to generalize from.
nuggets.quote: we about a year into it we actually we first we first started the company the first five engineers and me and Grant went to like a retreat for a weekend this is week one just to kind of talk about who we wanted to be as a company and we started thinking about values and goals and we kind of said we don't know who we are yet right we paused that and then a year later we came back to it ... because we can see everybody like 30 people or whatever at that period I came around it was about 30 people

nuggets.summary: Before co-founding Convoy, Lewis engineered a deliberate, multi-year career path specifically to earn technical credibility despite starting in management consulting rather than engineering. He describes roughly five or six sequential job moves — consulting to business development at a startup (which bought him startup credibility), to product management, to a role working directly with product engineering, to eventually managing engineering and product — each move calculated to close the gap toward a known end-state: enough credibility to be trusted by a technical co-founder and to be able to hire engineers himself. This is a specific, intentional career-sequencing strategy aimed at a predetermined future goal, not a generic "get more experience" narrative.
nuggets.quote: I made a bunch of moves over different companies to work my way back towards the engineering side of the house quitting going to a startup where I was in business development but that gave me some startup credibility which allowed me to go into a product management role which let me move into like go to a startup where I could then work directly with product engineering which then let me go into a role where I managed engineering and product like five or six roles later I finally got to a place where I felt like I was in a place where I could now have credibility on the engineering side to go work with a co-founder who trusts me

nuggets.summary: To validate the Convoy idea, Lewis ran a structured research process before ever talking to anyone in the industry: he researched the market and competitors online, then forced himself to write out every customer type and what each already pays money for (trucks, software, advice, etc.), combining these into several candidate business concepts written up like user stories. Only after narrowing to a few candidates did he go have field conversations with industry people, using those write-ups as a starting frame rather than going in fully open-ended. The process surfaced three genuinely distinct possible businesses within trucking, none matching his original desk-research hypotheses, and he picked one based on where people said they actually needed something rather than what he'd assumed going in.
nuggets.quote: we kind of came up with combinations of those that seemed like the most interesting and then wrote actual like paragraph or two this is a summary of what that business would be so we will do this for this customer almost like a user story ... it didn't turn out to be any of the things we wrote down but there were actually three distinct businesses we came up with within trucking and then we picked one of those three and they were very different

nuggets.summary: Lewis said working with investors hasn't been hard for him personally, but frames the relationship with a specific and somewhat counterintuitive warning: the moment they sign the first check is essentially the high point of the relationship, and it tends to get harder from there, not easier, once real difficulty hits. Because of that trajectory, he treats picking investors like dating, prioritizing genuine chemistry and the ability to have an open conversation during the easy early period, precisely because that same rapport gets tested later when things aren't going well. This runs counter to the assumption that investor relationships settle into smoother cooperation as trust builds over time — his experience is the reverse: the initial rapport is the peak, and everything after is harder until, maybe, a payoff moment much later.
nuggets.quote: I haven't found it to be hard to work with investors but I think that I you've got to pick the right investors you have to like actually it's dating like when you meet the person you really want to actually enjoy the conversation you have with them and feel like there's some natural relationship and chemistry there because you're in the best of times when you're raising money from them for the first time right someday when it isn't so easy ... it only gets harder from the time they're signing that first check
